content:
Consider a part of an electrical network as shown below. Some node voltages, and the current flowing through the $3 \Omega$ resistor are as indicated.

The voltage (in Volts) at node $X$ is __________ .
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Voltage at node } A \text { is }=9-(3 \times 1)=6 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \qquad I_D=\frac{8-6}{2+1}=\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{~A} \quad ; \quad V_x=8-\left(2 \times \frac{2}{3}\right)=\frac{20}{3} \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

content: In the given circuit, the current $I_x$ (in mA) is _______ .
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

Super node : If the voltage source (independent or dependent) is connected between two nonreference node then non-reference node form super node. Apply KCL for both nodes at a time.

$\Rightarrow \quad$ The voltage source inside the super node provides the equation necessary to solve for the node voltages.

$$ \text { Super node }=\mathrm{KCL}+\mathrm{KVL}+\text { Ohm's law } $$

Applying KCL (super node) :

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V_1}{1 \times 10^3}+\frac{V_2}{1 \times 10^3}+2 \mathrm{~mA}-5 \mathrm{~mA}=0 \\ & \frac{V_1+V_2}{1 \times 10^3}=3 \mathrm{~mA} \\ & V_1+V_2=3 \text { volt } \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Applying KVL (super node) :

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_2-V_1=1000 I_0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ & V_2-V_1=1000\left(\frac{V_1}{1 \times 10^3}\right) \\ & V_2=2 V_1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\end{aligned} $$

From equation (i)

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_2+\frac{V_2}{2}=3 \\ & 3 V_2=6 \\ & V_2=2 \mathrm{volt} \\ & I_x=\frac{V_2}{1 \times 10^3}=\frac{2}{1 \times 10^3}=2 \mathrm{~mA} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is 2 .

content: In the circuit shown below, the current i flowing through 200 $$\Omega$$ resistor is __________ mA (rounded off to two decimal places).
explanation:
Given circuit is,

Applying source transformation,

Applying nodal analysis at node V,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V-2}{2000}+\frac{V}{2000}-10^{-3}+\frac{V+1}{1200}=0 \\\\ & V=0.637 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

Current through $200 \Omega$ is,

$$ \begin{aligned} i_L & =\frac{V+1}{1200}=\frac{0.637+1}{1200} \\\\ i_L & =1.36 \mathrm{~mA} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is 1.36 .

content:
Consider the circuit shown in the figure.



The value of v0 (rounded off to one decimal place) is ______ V.
explanation:
Using Nodal analysis at node $V_0$

$$ \begin{aligned} &-6+\frac{V_0-4}{1}+\frac{V_0}{1}+8=0 \\\\ \Rightarrow &2 V_0-10+8=0 \\\\ \Rightarrow &2 V_0=2 \\\\ \Rightarrow &V_0=1 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

content: $$ \text { In the circuit shown below, the Thevenin voltage } V_{\text {th }} \text { is } $$
explanation:
$$ \text { Given circuit is shown in figure, } $$

We have to find open circuit voltage $V_{\text {th }}$.

Method 1

Using source transformation,

Applying KVL in loop 1,

$$ \begin{aligned} & 1-I(1)+2-2 I-2 I-4=0 \\ & 1+2-4=5 I \\ & I=-\frac{1}{5} \mathrm{Amp} \\ & V_{t h}=4+I(2)=4+\left(-\frac{1}{5}\right) \times 2=4-0.4=3.6 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

Method 2

By nodal method,

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Applying KCL at node } V_x \text {, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & -1+\frac{V_x}{1}+\frac{\left(V_x+2\right)-V_{t h}}{2}=0 \\ & 1 \mathrm{~A}=V_x+\left(\frac{V_x}{2}\right)+1-\frac{V_{t h}}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{3 V_x}{2}=\frac{V_{t h}}{2} \\ & V_x=\frac{V_{t h}}{3} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Applying KCL at node $V_{\text {th }}$,

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{~A}=\frac{V_{t h}-\left(V_x+2\right)}{2}+\frac{V_{t h}}{2} \\ & 4=V_{t h}-V_x-2+V_{t h} \\ & 4=2 V_{t h}-V_x-2 \\ & 2 V_{t h}-V_x=6 \end{aligned} $$

Putting the value of $V_x$ from equation (i),

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 V_{t h}-\frac{V_{t h}}{3}=6 \\ & \frac{6 V_{t h}-V_{t h}}{3}=6 \\ & \frac{5 V_{t h}}{3}=6 \\ & V_{t h}=\frac{6 \times 3}{5}=\frac{18}{5}=3.6 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content:
Consider the two-port resistive network shown in the figure. When an excitation of 5 V is applied across Port 1, and Port 2 is shorted, the current through the short circuit at Port 2 is measured to be 1 A (see (a) in the figure).

Now, if an excitation of 5 V is applied across Port 2, and Port 1 is shorted (see (b) in the figure), what is the current through the short circuit at Port 1?
explanation:
Applying KCL at node $V$ in figure (a)

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V-5}{1}+\frac{V}{R}+1=0 \quad \ldots \text { (i) } \\ & i_{2 \Omega}=1=\frac{V}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V=2 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

Substituting value of $V$ in equation (i),

$$ -3+\frac{2}{R}+1=0 $$

Therefore, $R=1 \Omega$

Now, applying KCL in figure (b),

$$ \frac{V-0}{1}+\frac{V}{1}+\frac{V-5}{2}=0 \Rightarrow V=1 \mathrm{~V} $$

Therefore, current through $1 \Omega$ resistor

$$ =\frac{V-0}{1}=1 \mathrm{~A} $$

Hence, the correct option is (C).

content:
In the circuit shown below, Vs is a constant voltage source and IL is a constant current load

The value of IL that maximizes the power absorbed by the constant current load is
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

We can replace constant current source $I_L$ with equivalent resistance $R_L$ and current flowing to the $R_L$ would be equal to $I_L$.

For maximum power transfer to the constant current source $I_L$, resistance of constant current source must be equal to source resistance i.e. $R$.

Applying KVL in above figure,

$$I_L=\frac{V_s}{2 R}$$

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: In the network shown in the figure, all resistors are identical with R = 300 $$\Omega$$. The resistance Rab (in $$\Omega$$) of the network is _______.
explanation:
The given network is in balanced mode, so redraw equivalent network.



$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad R_{a b} &=(2 R \| 2 R) \|(R \| R)=(R\|R\| R) \\\\ &=R / 3 \end{aligned} $$

But give $R=300 \Omega$

$$ \therefore \quad R_{a b}=100 \Omega $$

content: For the circuit shown in the figure, the Thevenin equivalent voltage (in Volts) across terminals a-b is _____.
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

Applying KVL in loop shown,

$$\begin{aligned} & -12-3(1-I)+6 I=0 \\ & -12-3+3 I+6 I=0 \\ & 9 I=15 \\ & I=\frac{15}{9}=\frac{5}{3} \mathrm{Amp} \end{aligned}$$

Hence, $V_{T H}=6 I=6 \times \frac{5}{3}=10$ Volt

Hence, the Thevenin's equivalent voltage across terminals $a-b$ is $\mathbf{1 0 ~ V}$.

content: In the circuit shown, the voltage Vx (in Volts) is ______.
explanation:
Applying KCL at point P in the circuit, we get

$${{{V_x}} \over {20}} + {{{V_x} - 0.25{V_x}} \over {10}} - 5 + 0.5{V_x} = 0$$

Therefore,

$${V_x}\left( {{1 \over {20}} + {{0.75} \over {10}} + 0.5} \right) = 5$$

Therefore, Vx = 8 V

options.content: in series with a current source
in parallel with a voltage source
in series with a voltage source
in parallel with a current source

explanation:
Norton equivalent circuit :

$R_N=R_{T H}=$ Equivalent resistance across load $R_L$ when replacing all other elements by their internal resistances.

Hence, the correct option is (D).

content: For maximum power transfer between two cascaded sections of an electrical network, the relationship between the output impedance Z<sub>1</sub> of the first section to the input impedance Z<sub>2</sub> of the second section is
explanation:
Two cascaded networks $N_1$ and $N_2$ are shown below,

If input impedance of $N$ is $Z_i$ and output impedance is $Z_0$.

Then equivalent circuit is shown below,

where, $V_i$ and $V_0$ are input and output voltages.

Output impedance of $1^{\text {st }}$ section is $Z_1$ and input impedance of $2^{\text {nd }}$ section is $Z_2$.

From $a b$ terminal the thevenin equivalent for network $N_1$.

Hence, $V_{T H}=V_1, \quad Z_{T H}=Z_{0_1}=Z_1$

Where load of $N_1, Z_{l_1}=Z_{i_2}=Z_2$

In case of complex network, maximum power will be transferred from $N_1$ to $N_2$, when

$Z_2=Z_1^*\qquad$ [Refer table]

Hence, the correct option is (C).

Maximum Power Transfer Theorem

S. No. $Z_S$ (Source Impedance) $Z_L$ (Load Impedance) Condition for MPT $P_L(\max )$ [Maximum power delivered to load] $$
\% \eta
$$

(Efficiency)
1. $R_S+j 0$ $R_L=R_S$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$ $50 \%$
2. $R_S+j X_S$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+\left(X_S+X_L\right)^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$
3. $R_S+j X_S$ $X_L+X_S=0$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2 R_L}{\left(R_s+R_L\right)^2}$ $<50 \%$
4. $R_S+j X_S$ $Z_L=Z_S^*$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$ $50 \%$
5. $R_S+j X_S$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+X_S^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$
6. $R_S+j 0$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+X_L^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$

content: The magnitude of current (in mA) through the resistor R2 in the figure shown is _____.
explanation:
Using source transformation, meaning, replacing the current source and parallel resistance into equivalent voltage source and series resistance,



Applying KVL in the above circuit, we get

$$ 20-2 \times 10^3 I-1 \times 10^3 I-4 \times 10^3 I+8-3 \times 10^3 I=0 $$

Therefore,

$$ I=\frac{28}{10 \times 10^3} \mathrm{~A}=2.8 \mathrm{~mA} $$

content: A source v<sub>s</sub>(t) = V cos 100 $$\pi$$t has an internal impedance of (4 + j3) $$\Omega$$. If a purely resistive load connected to this source has to extract the maximum power out of the source, its value in $$\Omega$$ shoud be
explanation:
According to question, circuit is shown below,

Internal impedance,

$$Z_S=R_S+j X_S=(4+j 3) \Omega$$

In case of complex network, maximum power will be transferred to $R_L$, when

$$\begin{aligned} & R_L=\left|Z_S\right|=\sqrt{R_S^2+X_S^2} \quad\text{[Refer table ] }\\ & X_L=0, \text { purely resistiv } \\ & R_L=\sqrt{4^2+3^2}=5 \Omega \\ & R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+X_S^2}=\left|Z_S\right| \\ & R_L=\sqrt{4^2+3^2}=5 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the correct option is (C).

Maximum Power Transfer Theorem

S. No. $Z_S$ (Source Impedance) $Z_L$ (Load Impedance) Condition for MPT $P_L(\max )$ [Maximum power delivered to load] $$
\% \eta
$$

(Efficiency)
1. $R_S+j 0$ $R_L=R_S$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$ $50 \%$
2. $R_S+j X_S$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+\left(X_S+X_L\right)^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$
3. $R_S+j X_S$ $X_L+X_S=0$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2 R_L}{\left(R_s+R_L\right)^2}$ $<50 \%$
4. $R_S+j X_S$ $Z_L=Z_S^*$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$ $50 \%$
5. $R_S+j X_S$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+X_S^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$
6. $R_S+j 0$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+X_L^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$

content: In the circuit shown below the value of RL such that the power transferred to RL is maximum
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

Thevenin's equivalent circuit :

In case of resistive network, maximum power will be transferred to $R_L$, when

$$R_L=R_{T H}$$

Calculation of $\boldsymbol{R}_{T H}$ :

(when dependent sources are not present)

Replace all independent source by their internal resistance, i.e. short circuit all the independent voltage source ( $R_{i n}=0$ ) and open circuit all the independent current source $\left(R_{\text {in }}=\infty\right)$.

$$\begin{aligned} & R_{T H}=(10 \| 10)+10=5+10 \\ & R_{T H}=15 \Omega \\ & R_L=R_{T H}=15 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

content: In the circuit shown below, the Norton equivalent current in amperes with respect to terminals P and Q is
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below.

The Norton equivalent current $I_N$ across $P Q$ is the short circuit current across $P Q$.

There is no current in $-j 50 \Omega$, because of short circuit path $P Q$.

$$\begin{aligned} & I_N=16 \angle 0^0 \times \frac{25}{25+(15+j 30)}[\mathrm{By} \mathrm{CDR}] \\ & I_N=\frac{400}{40+j 30}=\frac{40}{4+j 3} \\ & I_N=\frac{40(4-j 3)}{25}=1.6(4-j 3) \\ & I_N=(6.4-j 4.8) \mathrm{A} \end{aligned}$$

content: A fully charged mobile phone with a 12 V battery is good for a 10 minute talk-time. Assume that, during the talk-time, the battery delivers a constant current of 2 A and its voltage drops linearly from 12 V to 10 V as shown in the figure. How much energy does the battery deliver during this talk-time?
explanation:
We know that

$$ P=v(t) \cdot i(t) $$

Energy

$$ P \cdot t=v(t) \cdot i(t) \cdot t $$

Now, $i(t)=I=2 A$ (given)

and $v(t) \cdot t=$ Area under $v(t)-t$ curve. Therefore,

$$ \begin{aligned} v(t) \cdot t &=\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 600\right)+(10 \times 600) \\\\ &=600+6000=6600 \end{aligned} $$

Therefore,

$$ E=(6600) \times 2 \mathrm{~J}=13200 \mathrm{~J}=13.2 \mathrm{~kJ} $$

content: The maximum power that can be transferred to the load resistor RL from the voltage source in figure is
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

In figure (a), the independent voltage source is connected between terminal $a$ and $b$ and the current is shown between $c$ and $d$.

There is resistive network between $a b$ and $c d$. Hence, it is linear as well as reciprocal.

(i) According to reciprocity theorem, Source and response can be inter changed.

(ii) Using linearity property, If input is doubled i.e. 20 V , the output (response) also gets doubled i.e. 4 A.

(iii) If polarity of input 20 V is reversed then in output, direction of current is also reversed.

Comparing fig. (4) and fig. (b),

Hence, $i=-4 \mathrm{~A}$

content: A source of angular frequency 1rad/sec has source impedance consisting of $$1\Omega$$ resistance in series with 1H inductance. The load that will obtain maximum power transfer is
explanation:
According to the question, circuit diagram is shown below,

In case of complex network, maximum power will be transferred to $Z_L$, when

$$\begin{aligned} & Z_L=Z_S^* \quad\text{[Refer Table]}\\ & Z_L=(1+j)^*=(1-j) \Omega \end{aligned}$$

Since, load reactance is negative it means it is a capacitive element.

$$X_C=\frac{1}{\omega C}=1 \Rightarrow C=1 \mathrm{~F}$$

$(1-j) \Omega$ is equivalent to $1 \Omega$ resistance in series with 1 F capacitor.

Maximum Power Transfer Theorem

S. No. $Z_S$ (Source Impedance) $Z_L$ (Load Impedance) Condition for MPT $P_L(\max )$ [Maximum power delivered to load] $$
\% \eta
$$

(Efficiency)
1. $R_S+j 0$ $R_L=R_S$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$ $50 \%$
2. $R_S+j X_S$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+\left(X_S+X_L\right)^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$
3. $R_S+j X_S$ $X_L+X_S=0$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2 R_L}{\left(R_s+R_L\right)^2}$ $<50 \%$
4. $R_S+j X_S$ $Z_L=Z_S^*$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$ $50 \%$
5. $R_S+j X_S$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+X_S^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$
6. $R_S+j 0$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+X_L^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$

content: Superposition theorem is NOT applicable to networks containing
explanation:
The Superposition Theorem is a fundamental principle in linear circuit analysis. It states that in any linear, bilateral network with multiple independent sources, the total response (voltage or current) in any component is the algebraic sum of the responses caused by each independent source acting alone, while all other independent sources are turned off (voltage sources replaced by short circuits, and current sources replaced by open circuits).

The key requirement for the Superposition Theorem to be applicable is linearity.

Let's examine the options:

  • A. Nonlinear elements: Nonlinear elements (like diodes, transistors, varistors, etc.) do not have a linear relationship between voltage and current. If a circuit contains nonlinear elements, the principle of superposition does not hold, because the effects of individual sources cannot simply be added together to find the total effect. For example, doubling an input voltage to a diode does not simply double its current linearly.

  • B. Dependent voltage sources: Dependent sources (voltage-controlled voltage sources, current-controlled current sources, etc.) are linear components. They do not violate the linearity requirement. When applying superposition, dependent sources are not treated as independent sources; they are kept active in the circuit and respond to the voltages/currents present due to the single active independent source. Therefore, superposition is applicable to networks containing dependent voltage sources.

  • C. Dependent current sources: Similar to dependent voltage sources, dependent current sources are also linear components. Superposition is applicable to networks containing them, as they are kept active during the analysis.

  • D. Transformers: An ideal transformer is a linear, passive, and bilateral device. Even real transformers, when modeled linearly (ignoring saturation or hysteresis), do not introduce non-linearity that would prevent the use of superposition. The relationships between primary and secondary voltages and currents are linear.

Therefore, the Superposition Theorem is NOT applicable to networks containing Nonlinear elements.

The final answer is $\boxed{A}$

content: If the secondary winding of the ideal transformer shown in the circuit of the figure has 40 turns, the number of turns in the primary winding for maximum power transfer to the 2 Ω resistor will be
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

Let the number of turns in the primary and secondary winding be $n_1$ and $n_2$.

Turns ratio, $n=\frac{n_1}{n_2}=\frac{n_1}{40}$

Modified circuit is given by,

where, reflected impedance $R_L{ }^{\prime}=n^2 R_L$

$$R_L^{\prime}=2 n^2$$

For maximum power transfer,

$$\begin{aligned} & R_L^{\prime}=R_S=8 \Omega \\ & 2 n^2=8 \\ & n^2=\frac{8}{2}=4 \\ & n=\frac{n_1}{n_2}=\sqrt{4}=2 \\ & \frac{n_1}{40}=2 \\ & n_1=80 \text { turns } \end{aligned}$$

content:
In the network shown below, maximum power is to be transferred to the load $R_L$.

The value of $R_L$ (in Ω) is ______.
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below.

Calculation of $R_{T H}$ across the Load $R_L$ : (When dependent sources are present)

Apply a voltage source $V_{d c}$ between the Load $R_L$ and assume $I_{d c}$ current flowing to the Network.

Replace all Independent sources by their respective internal resistance i.e. short circuit all the Independent voltage source ( $R_{i n}=0$ ) and open circuit all the Independent current source ( $R_{\text {in }}=\infty$ )

From the circuit diagram :

Applying KVL in Loop (1) :

$$ \begin{aligned} & -V_{d c}+2\left(I_{d c}-\frac{V_x}{3}\right)+V_x=0 \\ & -V_{d c}+2 I_{d c}-\frac{2 V_x}{3}+V_x=0 \end{aligned} $$

$$ -V_{d c}+2 I_{d c}+\frac{V_x}{3}=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Applying KVL in Loop (2) :

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_x+\frac{2 V_x}{3}-V_x-2\left(I_{d c}-\frac{V_x}{3}\right)=0 \\ & \frac{2 V_x}{3}-2 I_{d c}+\frac{2 V_x}{3}=0 \\ & 2 I_{d c}=\frac{4 V_x}{3} \\ & V_x=\frac{6 I_{d c}}{4} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

From equation (i)

$$ \begin{aligned} & -V_{d c}+2 I_{d c}+\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{6}{4} I_{d c}\right)=0 \\ & -V_{d c}+2 I_{d c}+\frac{1}{2} I_{d c}=0 \\ & V_{d c}=\frac{5}{2} I_{d c} \\ & R_{T H}=\frac{V_{d c}}{I_{d c}}=\frac{5}{2}=2.5 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

Condition for MPT :

$$ R_L=R_{T H}=2.5 \Omega $$

Hence the correct answer is $\mathbf{2 . 5} \boldsymbol{\Omega}$.

content:
Consider the circuit shown in the figure.

The Thevenin equivalent resistance (in Ω) across P – Q is _________.
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

Calculation of $R_{T H}$ :

(when dependent sources are present)

(i) Apply a voltage source ' $V_{d c}$ ' at terminal of $R_L$, and assume ' $I_{d c}$ ' current is flowing to the network.

(ii) Replace all independent source by their internal resistance, i.e. short circuit all the independent voltage source ( $R_{i s}=0$ ) and open circuit all the independent current sources $\left(R_{l m}=\infty\right)$.

$R_{T H}=\frac{V_{d c}}{I_{d c}}=\frac{i_0 \times 1}{I_{d c}}=\frac{i_0}{I_{d c}}\quad\text{.... (i)}$

Applying KVL in loop (1),

$$\begin{aligned} & -3 i_0+i_0 \times 1+\left(i_0-I_{d c}\right) \times 1=0 \\ & I_{d c}=-i_0 \\ & \frac{i_0}{I_{d c}}=-1 \end{aligned}$$

Hence, from equation (i),

$$R_{T H}=-1 \Omega$$

Hence, the Thevenin equivalent resistance across $P Q$ is $-1 \Omega$.

content: In the circuit shown in the figure, the maximum power (in watt) delivered to the resistor R is _________.
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

Thevenin equivalent circuit across R is given by,

In case of resistive network, maximum power will be delivered to $R$, when

$$R_{T H}=R$$

Maximum power is given by,

$$P_{\max }=\frac{V_{T H}^2}{4 R_{T H}}\quad\text{..... (i)}$$

1. Calculation of $V_{T H}$ :

By VDR in loop (I),

$$v_0=\frac{2}{2+3} \times 5=2 \mathrm{~V}$$

By VDR in loop (II),

$$\begin{aligned} & V_{T H}=\frac{40}{40+10} \times 100 v_0 \\ & V_{T H}=\frac{40}{50} \times 100 \times 2=160 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$$

2. Calculation of $R_{T H}$ :

(when dependent sources are present)

(i) Replace all independent source by their internal resistance, i.e. short circuit all the independent voltage source ( $R_{i n}=0$ ) and open circuit all the independent current source ( $R_{\text {in }}=\infty$ ).

(ii) Short the independent voltage source of 5 volt.

$v_0=0$ [From figure]

Hence dependent voltage source $100 v_0=0$.

(iii) Both dependent and independent voltage source will acts as a short circuit.

$$\begin{aligned} & R_{T H}=10 \mathrm{k} \Omega \| 40 \mathrm{k} \Omega=\frac{40 \times 10}{40+10} \mathrm{k} \Omega \\ & R_{T H}=\frac{400}{50}=8 \mathrm{k} \Omega=8000 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

From equation (i),

$$P_{\max }=\frac{V_{T H}^2}{4 R_{T H}}=\frac{160 \times 160}{4 \times 8000}=0.8 \mathrm{~W}$$

Hence, the maximum power delivered to the resistor $R$ is $\mathbf{0 . 8} \mathbf{~ W}$.

content: In the circuit shown, the Norton equivalent resistance (in Ω) across terminals a–b is ___________.
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

Norton equivalent resistance represents thevenin equivalent resistance.

Calculation of $\boldsymbol{R}_{T H}$ :

(when dependent sources are present)

(i) Apply a voltage source ' $V_{d c}$ ' at terminal $a b$ and assume ' $I_{d c}$ ' current flowing to the network.

(ii) Replace all independent source by their internal resistance, i.e. short circuit all the independent voltage source ( $R_{\text {in }}=0$ ) and open circuit all the independent current source $\left(R_{i n}=\infty\right)$.

Applying KVL at node $a$,

$$\frac{V_{d c}-4 I}{2}+\frac{V_{d c}}{2}+\frac{V_{d c}}{4}-I_{d c}=0$$

where, $I=\frac{V_{d c}}{4}$ [From figure]

Hence,

$$\begin{aligned} & 4 I=V_{d c} \\ & \frac{V_{d c}-V_{d c}}{2}+\frac{V_{d c}}{2}+\frac{V_{d c}}{4}=I_{d c} \\ & \frac{3 V_{d c}}{4}=I_{d c} \\ & \frac{V_{d c}}{I_{d c}}=R_{T H}=R_N=\frac{4}{3} \Omega=1.33 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the Norton equivalent resistance across terminals $a-b$ is $\mathbf{1 . 3 3} \boldsymbol{\Omega}$.

content: In the circuit shown in the figure, the value of node voltage V2 is
explanation:
Applying KCL at node V1,

$$ - 4\angle 0^\circ = {{{V_1}} \over { - j3}} + {{{V_1} - {V_2}} \over 4} + I = 0$$ ..... (i)

Applying KCL at node V2,

$${{{V_2}} \over {j6}} + {{{V_2}} \over 6} + {{{V_2} - {V_1}} \over 4} - I = 0$$ ..... (ii)

Appling KVL for V1 and V2 , we get

$${V_1} - {V_2} = 10\angle 0^\circ $$ [From figure]

$${V_1} = {V_2} + 10$$ .... (iii)

From equation (i) and (ii),

$$ - 4\angle 0^\circ + {{{V_1}} \over { - j3}} + {{{V_1} - {V_2}} \over 4} + {{{V_2}} \over {j6}} + {{{V_2}} \over 6} + {{{V_2} - {V_1}} \over 4} = 0$$

$${V_1}\left[ {{{ - 1} \over {j3}} + {1 \over 4} - {1 \over 4}} \right] + {V_2}\left[ {{{ - 1} \over 4} + {1 \over {j6}} + {1 \over 6} + {1 \over 4}} \right] = 4$$

$$j{{{V_1}} \over 3} + {1 \over 6}(1 - j){V_2} = 4$$ .... (iv)

From equation (iii) and (iv),

$${j \over 3}({V_2} + 10) + {1 \over 6}(1 - j){V_2} = 4$$

$${V_2}\left[ {{j \over 3} + {1 \over 6} - {j \over 6}} \right] = 4 - {{j10} \over 3}$$

$${{{V_2}} \over 6}(1 + j) = 4 - {{j10} \over 3}$$

$${V_2} = {{2(12 - j10)} \over {1 + j}} = {{4(6 - j5)} \over {1 + j}}$$

$${V_2} = {{4(6 - j5)(1 - j)} \over {(1 + j)(1 - j)}}$$

$${V_2} = 2\left[ {(6 - 5) - j11} \right] = (2 - j22)\,V$$

content: In the circuit shown in the figure, the angular frequency $$\omega$$ (in rad/s), at which the Norton equivalent impedance as seen from terminals b-b' is purely resistive, is _______.
In the circuit shown below, if the source voltage Vs =100$$\angle$$53.130V then the Thevenin’s equivalent voltage in Volts as seen by the load resistance RL is
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

Thevenin's equivalent voltage across $R_L$ is $V_{T H}$. where, $V_{T H}=$ open circuit voltage across $R_L$ i.e. $I_2=0$.

Then the dependent voltage source

$$=j 40 I_2=0 \text { (S.C.) }$$

Current $I_1$ in loop is given by,

$$\begin{aligned} & I_1=\frac{V_S}{3+j 4}=\frac{100 \angle 53.13^0}{3+j 4} \\ & I_1=\frac{100 \angle 53.13^0}{5 \angle 53.13^0}=20 \angle 0^0 \mathrm{~A} \\ & V_{L_1}=j 4 I_1=j 4 \times 20 \angle 0^0=80 \angle 90^0 \mathrm{~V} \\ & V_{T H}=10 V_{L_1}=800 \angle 90^0 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the correct option is (C).

content: The following arrangement consists of an ideal transformer and an attenuator which attenuates by a factor of 0.8. An ac voltage Vwx1 = 100V is applied across WX to get an open circuit voltage YZ1 V across YZ. Next, an ac voltage VYZ2 =100V is applied across YZ to get an open circuit voltage VWX2 across WX. Then, VYZ1 / VWX1 , VWX2 / VYZ2 are respectively,

With 10 V dc connected at port A in the linear nonreciprocal two-port network shown below, the following were observed:
(i) 1Ω connected at port B draws a current of 3 A
(ii) 2.5Ω connected at port B draws a current of 2 A

With 10 V dc connected at port A, the current drawn by 7Ω connected at port B is
explanation:
$$ \text { Given circuit is shown below, } $$

$$ \text { By using Thevenin's equivalent : } $$

$$ \text { Thevenin's equivalent circuit : } $$

Current $I$ is given by,

$$ I=\frac{V_{T H}}{R_{T H}+R_L} $$

  1. 1. When $R_L=1 \Omega$

$$ \begin{aligned} & I=3 \mathrm{~A} \\ & V_{T H}=3 R_{T H}+3 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

  1. 2. When $R_L=2.5 \Omega$

$$ \begin{aligned} & I=2 \mathrm{~A} \\ & V_{T H}=2 R_{T H}+5 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

From equation (i) and (ii),

$$ \begin{aligned} & R_{T H}=2 \Omega \\ & V_{T H}=9 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

When $R_L=7 \Omega$

$$ I=\frac{V_{T H}}{R_{T H}+R_L}=\frac{9}{2+7}=1 \mathrm{~A} $$

Hence, the correct option is (C).

content: Assuming both the voltage sources are in phase the value of R for which maximum power is transferred from circuit A to circuit B is
explanation:
Maximum power is transferred from circuit $A$ to circuit $B$, if circuit $B$ offers $2 \Omega$ to circuit $A$.

Thevenin equivalent of circuit A is given by,

Then,

$$\begin{aligned} & I=\frac{10}{4}=2.5 \mathrm{~A} \\ \text{and}\quad & V_{P Q}=I \times 2=2.5 \times 2 \\ & V_{P Q}=5 \mathrm{Volt} \\ & I_R=\frac{5-3}{R}=\frac{2}{R} \end{aligned}$$

and

$$\begin{aligned} & I_R=I \quad\text{[From figure]}\\ & \frac{2}{R}=2.5 \\ & R=\frac{2}{2.5}=0.8 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content:
With 10 V dc connected at port A in the linear nonreciprocal two-port network shown below, the following were observed:
(i) 1Ω connected at port B draws a current of 3 A
(ii) 2.5Ω connected at port B draws a current of 2 A

For the same network, with 6 V dc connected at port A, 1Ω connected at port B draws 7/3 A. If 8 V dc is connected to port A, the open circuit voltage at port B is
explanation:
$V_{T H}$ depends on independent voltage source and varies with applied voltage. But $R_{T H}$ does not depend on voltage source, it is same for all voltage source because while calculating $R_{T H}$, voltage source is short circuited. $R_{T H}$ depends on element of network.

For 6 V voltage source connected at port $\boldsymbol{A}$ :

$$ R_L=1 \Omega, \quad I=\frac{7}{3} \mathrm{~A} $$

and $\quad R_{T H}=2 \Omega$ (from previous question)

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_{T H} \neq 9 \mathrm{~V} \quad \text { (because voltage source is } \\ & \quad \text { changed from } 10 \mathrm{~V} \text { to } 6 \mathrm{~V} \text { ) } \\ & V_{T H}=I\left(R_{T H}+R_L\right) \end{aligned} $$

(From Thevenin equivalent circuit)

$$ V_{T H}=\frac{7}{3}(1+2)=7 \mathrm{~V} $$

For 8 V voltage source connected at port $\boldsymbol{A}$ :

Given : The network is linear and nonreciprocal.

Hence, network may contain dependent sources.

So, $\quad V_{T H}=\alpha V+\beta$

When $V=6 \mathrm{~V}, V_{T H}=7 \mathrm{~V}$

$$ 7=6 \alpha+\beta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

When $V=10 \mathrm{~V}, V_{T H}=9 \mathrm{~V}$

$$ 9=10 \alpha+\beta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)$$

From equation (i) and (ii)

$$ \begin{aligned} & \alpha=\frac{1}{2}, \beta=4 \\ & V_{T H}=\frac{V}{2}+4 \end{aligned} $$

For $V=8 \mathrm{~V}$

$$ V_{T H}=\frac{8}{2}+4=8 \mathrm{~V} $$

(Open circuit voltage of port $B$ )

Hence, the correct option is (C).

content: In the circuit shown, what value of RL maximizes the power delivered to RL?
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

Thevenin's equivalent circuit :

In case of resistive network, maximum power will be transferred to $R_L$, when

$$R_L=R_{T H}$$

Calculation of $\boldsymbol{R}_{T H}$ :

(when dependent sources are present)

(i) Apply a voltage source ' $V_{d c}$ ' at terminal $a b$ and assume ' $I_{d c}$ ' current flowing to the network.

(ii) Replace all independent source by their internal resistance, i.e. short circuit all the independent voltage source ( $R_{i n}=0$ ) and open circuit all the independent current source ( $R_{\text {in }}=\infty$ ).

Modified circuit is shown in below figure,

Applying KCL at node $a$,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{V_{d c}-V_x}{4}+\frac{V_{d c}}{8}-I_{d c}=0 \\ & 2\left(V_{d c}-V_x\right)+V_{d c}=8 I_{d c} \\ & 3 V_{d c}-2 V_x=8 I_{d c}\quad\text{..... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Current, $i=\frac{V_{d c}}{8}=\frac{V_x}{4}\qquad$ [From figure]

and $V_x=4 i=\frac{V_{d c}}{2}\qquad$ .... (ii)

From equation (i) and (ii),

$$\begin{aligned} & 3 V_{d c}-V_{d c}=8 I_{d c} \\ & \frac{V_{d c}}{I_{d c}}=4 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

So, $\quad R_{T H}=4 \Omega$

content: The Thevenin equivalent impedance Zth between the nodes P and Q in the following circuit is
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

For DC source under steady state, inductor behaves as a short circuit and capacitor behaves as an open circuit.

Modified circuit under steady state condition can be drawn as shown below,

Calculation of $Z_{T H}$ :

(when dependent sources are not present)

Replace all independent source by their internal resistance, i.e. short circuit all the independent voltage source ( $R_{\text {in }}=0$ ) and open circuit all the independent current source ( $R_{i n}=\infty$ ).

content: For the circuit shown in the figure, the Thevenin voltage and resistance looking into X-Y are:
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

1. Calculation of $V_{T H}$ :

Applying KCL at node $a$,

$$-2+\frac{V_{T H}}{2}+\frac{V_{T H}}{1}+\frac{V_{T H}-2 i}{1}=0$$

where, $i=\frac{V_{T H}}{1}$

$$\begin{aligned} & 2=\frac{V_{T H}}{2}+\frac{V_{T H}}{1}+\frac{V_{T H}-2 V_{T H}}{1} \\ & 2=\frac{V_{T H}}{2}+\frac{V_{T H}}{1}-\frac{V_{T H}}{1} \\ & V_{T H}=4 \mathrm{Volt} \end{aligned}$$

2. Calculation of $R_{T H}$ :

(when dependent sources are present)

(i) Apply a voltage source ' $V_{d c}$ ' at terminal $a b$ and assume ' $I_{d c}$ ' current flowing to the network.

(ii) Replace all independent source by their internal resistance, i.e. short circuit all the independent voltage source ( $R_{\text {in }}=0$ ) and open circuit all the independent current source ( $R_{i n}=\infty$ ).

Applying KCL at node $a$,

$$-I_{d c}+\frac{V_{d c}}{2}+\frac{V_{d c}}{1}+\frac{V_{d c}-2 i}{1}=0$$

where,

$$\begin{aligned} & i=\frac{V_{d c}}{1}=V_{d c} \\ & I_{d c}=\frac{V_{d c}}{2}+\frac{V_{d c}}{1}+\frac{V_{d c}-2 V_{d c}}{1} \\ & I_{d c}=\frac{V_{d c}}{2} \\ & \frac{V_{d c}}{I_{d c}}=R_{T H}=2 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

content: For the circuit shown in figure, Thevenin’s voltage and Thevenin’s equivalent resistance at terminals a – b is
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

1. Calculation of $V_{T H}$ :

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Applying } \mathrm{KCL} \text { at node } c \text {, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & -1+\frac{V_{T H}}{5}+\frac{V_{T H}-10}{5}=0 \\ & 1=\frac{2 V_{T H}}{5}-2 \\ & \frac{2 V_{T H}}{5}=3 \\ & V_{T H}=\frac{15}{2}=7.5 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

2. Calculation of $R_{T H}$ :

(when dependent sources are present)

(i) Apply a voltage source ' $V_{d c}$ ' at terminal $a b$ and assume ' $I_{d c}$ ' current is flowing to the network.

(ii) Replace all independent source by their internal resistance, i.e. short circuit all the independent voltage source ( $R_{i n}=0$ ) and open circuit all the independent current source ( $R_{i n}=\infty$ ).

Applying KCL at node $a$,

$$-I_{d c}+\frac{V_{d c}}{5}+I_1=0 \text { and } I_1=\frac{V_{d c}}{5}$$

Hence,

$$\begin{aligned} & -I_{d c}+\frac{V_{d c}}{5}+\frac{V_{d c}}{5}=0 \\ & I_{d c}=\frac{2 V_{d c}}{5} \\ & \frac{V_{d c}}{I_{d c}}=R_{T H}=2.5 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

content: In the network of Figure, the maximum power is delivered to RL if its value is
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

In case of resistive network, maximum power will be delivered to $R_L$, when

$$R_L=R_{T H}$$

Calculation of $\boldsymbol{R}_{T H}$ :

(when dependent sources are present)

(i) Apply a voltage source ' $V_{d c}$ ' at terminal of $R_L$, and assume ' $I_{d c}$ ' current is flowing to the network.

(ii) Replace all independent source by their internal resistance, i.e. short circuit all the independent voltage sources ( $R_{i n}=0$ ) and open circuit all the independent current sources $\left(R_{i n}=\infty\right)$.

Applying KCL at node $a$,

$$-I_{d c}-0.5 I_1+I_1+\frac{V_{d c}}{20}=0$$

where, $I_1=\frac{V_{d c}}{40}$

$$\begin{aligned} & I_{d c}+0.5 \times \frac{V_{d c}}{40}=\frac{V_{d c}}{40}+\frac{V_{d c}}{20} \\ & I_{d c}+\frac{V_{d c}}{80}=\frac{3 V_{d c}}{40} \\ & I_{d c}=\frac{3 V_{d c}}{40}-\frac{V_{d c}}{80} \\ & I_{d c}=\frac{5 V_{d c}}{80} \\ & \frac{V_{d c}}{I_{d c}}=R_{T H}=R_L=\frac{80}{5}=16 \Omega\quad\text{..... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

content: In figure, the value of the load resistor R which maximizes the power delivered to it is
explanation:
Given : $\omega=10 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}\quad$ [From AC source]

$$X_S=\omega L=10 \Omega, R_S=10 \Omega$$

Circuit is shown below,

Here, $Z_S=(10+j 10) \Omega$

In case of complex network, maximum power will be transferred to $R_L$, when

$$\begin{aligned} & R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+X_S^2} \quad\text{[Refer Table]}\\ & R_L=\sqrt{10^2+10^2}=10 \sqrt{2} \Omega \\ & R_L=14.14 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

Maximum Power Transfer Theorem

S. No. $Z_S$ (Source Impedance) $Z_L$ (Load Impedance) Condition for MPT $P_L(\max )$ [Maximum power delivered to load] $$
\% \eta
$$

(Efficiency)
1. $R_S+j 0$ $R_L=R_S$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$ $50 \%$
2. $R_S+j X_S$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+\left(X_S+X_L\right)^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$
3. $R_S+j X_S$ $X_L+X_S=0$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2 R_L}{\left(R_s+R_L\right)^2}$ $<50 \%$
4. $R_S+j X_S$ $Z_L=Z_S^*$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$ $50 \%$
5. $R_S+j X_S$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+X_S^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$
6. $R_S+j 0$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+X_L^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$

content: The voltage e0 in figure is
Use the data of Fig.(a). The current i in the circuit of Fig.(b) is
explanation:
$$ \text { Given circuit is shown below, } $$

Method 1

In figure (a), the independent voltage source is connected between terminal $a$ and $b$ and the current is shown between $c$ and $d$.

There is resistive network between $a b$ and $c d$. Hence, it is linear as well as reciprocal.

(i) According to reciprocity theorem, Source and response can be inter changed.

(ii) Using linearity property,

If input is doubled i.e. 20 V , the output (response) also gets doubled i.e. 4 A .

(iii) If polarity of input 20 V is reversed then in output, direction of current is also reversed.

Comparing fig. (4) and fig. (b), Hence, $i=-4 \mathrm{~A}$

Hence, the correct option is (C).

content: The value of R (in ohms) required for maximum power transfer in the network shown in Fig. is
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

Thevenin's equivalent circuit :

In case of resistive network, maximum power will transferred to R when, $R=R_{TH}$

Calculation of $\boldsymbol{R}_{T H}$ :

(when dependent sources are not present) Replace all independent source by their internal resistance, i.e. short circuit all the independent voltage source ( $R_{\text {in }}=0$ ) and open circuit all the independent current source ( $R_{\text {in }}=\infty$ ).

$$\begin{aligned} & R_{T H}=(5 \| 20)+4=4+4 \\ & R_{T H}=8 \Omega \\ & R=R_{T H}=8 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

content: The Thevenin equivalent voltage VTH appearing between the terminals A and B of the network shown in Fig. is given by
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

Calculation of $V_{T H}$ :

Thevenin voltage $V_{T H}$ is equal to the voltage across $j 4$ impedance.

$$\begin{aligned} & V_{T H}=100 \angle 0^0 \times \frac{j 4}{(j 4+3)}=\frac{j 400}{j 4+3} \\ & V_{T H}=\frac{j 400}{3+j 4} \times \frac{(3-j 4)}{(3-j 4)}=\frac{j 400(3-j 4)}{25} \\ & V_{T H}=j 16(3-j 4) \end{aligned}$$

content: A DC circuit shown in the figure has a voltage source V, a current source I and several resistors. A particular resistor R dissipates a power of 4 Watts when V alone is active. The same resistor R dissipates a power of 9 Watts when I alone is active. The power dissipated by R when both sources are active will be
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

(i) Let voltage source $\boldsymbol{V}$ alone is active :

Replace current source by its internal resistance. i.e. $R=\infty$ (O.C.)

Current through resistor $R$ is $I_1$.

Power dissipated in resistor $R$ is given by,

$$ P_1=I_1^2 R=4 \mathrm{~W} $$

(ii) Let current source $\boldsymbol{I}$ alone is active :

Replace voltage source by its internal resistance. i.e. $R=0$ (S.C.)

Current through resistor $R$ is $I_2$.

Power dissipated in resistor $R$ is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & P_2=I_2^2 R=9 \mathrm{~W} \\ & P_1 P_2=I_1^2 R \times I_2^2 R=(4 \times 9) \mathrm{W}=36 \mathrm{~W} \\ & \sqrt{P_1 P_2}=I_1 I_2 R=\sqrt{36}=6 \mathrm{~W} \end{aligned} $$

(iii) When both sources are active :

According to superposition theorem, current through resistor $R$ is,

$I=I_1+I_2 \quad\left(\right.$ when $I_1$ and $I_2$ are in same direction)

$I=I_1-I_2 \quad$ (when $I_1$ and $I_2$ are in opposite direction)

Total power is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & P=\left(I_1 \pm I_2\right)^2 R=I_1^2 R+I_2^2 R \pm 2 I_1 I_2 R \\ & P=P_1+P_2 \pm 2 \sqrt{P_1 P_2} \\ & P=4+9 \pm 12=25 \mathrm{~W} \text { or } 1 \mathrm{~W} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct options are (A) and (D).

content: In the circuit of figure, when switch S1 is closed, the ideal ammeter M1 reads 5A. What will be ideal voltmeter M2 read when S1 is kept open? (The value of E is not specified).
explanation:
Ideal ammeter measures the short circuit current when switch is closed.

Ideal voltmeter measures the open circuit voltage when switch is open.

Given : When $S_1$ is closed, then reading of $M_1=5 \mathrm{~A}$.

This current is equal to short circuit current, $I_{S C}=5 \mathrm{~A}$ across $S_1$.

The reading of ideal voltmeter $M_2$, when $S_1$ is kept open, is equal to the open circuit voltage $V_{O C}$.

Circuit is shown below,

Calculation of $\boldsymbol{R}_{T H}$ :

(when dependent sources are not present)

Replace all independent source by their internal resistance, i.e. short circuit all the independent voltage source ( $R_{i n}=0$ ) and open circuit all the independent current source ( $R_{i n}=\infty$ ).

$$\begin{aligned} & R_{T H}=10 \| 10+5=5+5 \\ & R_{T H}=10 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

Then open circuit voltage $V_{O C}$ is given by,

$$\begin{aligned} & V_{O C}=I_{S C} R_{T H} \\ & V_{O C}=5 \times 10=50 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the ideal voltmeter $M_2$ read when $S_1$ is kept open is $\mathbf{5 0 ~ V}$.

content: In the circuit of figure, the power dissipated in the register R is 1W when only source '1' is present and '2' replaced by a short.The power dissipated in the same registor R is 4W when only source '2' is present and '1' is replaced by a short.When both the source '1' and '2' are present, the power dissipated in R will be:
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below.

Given : When only source ' 1 ' is present then $P_1=1 \mathrm{~W}$.

When only source ' 2 ' is present then $P_2=4 \mathrm{~W}$.

Let current through resistor $R$ is $I_1$ from $b$ to $a$ terminal, when only source 1 is present.

Current through $R$ is $I_2$ from $a$ to $b$ terminal, when only source 2 is present.

By using super position theorem :

When both sources are present the current will be ( $I_1-I_2$ ), because polarity of voltage sources are opposite.

Hence total power is given by,

$$\begin{aligned} & P=\left(I_1-I_2\right)^2 R \\ & P=I_1^2 R+I_2^2 R-2 I_1 I_2 R \\ & P=P_1+P_2-2 \sqrt{P_1 P_2} \\ & P=\left(\sqrt{P_1}-\sqrt{P_2}\right)^2 \\ & P=(\sqrt{1}-\sqrt{4})^2=(1-2)^2 \\ & P=1 \mathrm{~W} \end{aligned}$$

content: A load, ZL = RL + jXL is to be matched, using an ideal transformer, to a generator of internal impedance, ZS = RS + jXS. The turns ratio of the transformer required is
explanation:
$$ \text { According to the question, } $$

Reflected impedance is given by,

$$ Z_L^{\prime}=n^2 Z_L \quad \text { where, } n=\frac{n_1}{n_2} $$

Turn ratio $=\frac{n_1}{n_2}$

For maximum power transfer,

$$ \begin{aligned} & Z_L^{\prime}=Z_S^* \\ & n^2 Z_L=Z_S^* \\ & n^2=\frac{Z_S^*}{Z_L} \end{aligned} $$

Required turn ratio of transformer is given by,

$$ \frac{n_1}{n_2}=n=\sqrt{\frac{Z_S^*}{Z_L}} $$

$n$ is a positive integer.

$$ n=\sqrt{\left|\frac{Z_S}{Z_L}\right|} $$

where, $\left|Z_S{ }^*\right|=\left|Z_S\right|$

Since, $\frac{n_1}{n_2}=\sqrt{\left|\frac{Z_S}{Z_L}\right|}$

content: If an impedance ZL is connected across a voltage source V with source impedance ZS, then for maximum power transfer, the load impedance must be equal to
explanation:
According to maximum power transfer theorem, load impedance should be equal to complex conjugate of source impedance.

$$\begin{aligned} & R_S=R_L \text { and } X_L=-X_S \quad \text{[when both $R_L$ and $X_L$ are varying]}\\ & Z_L=R_L+j X_L \\ & Z_S^*=R_S-j X_S \end{aligned}$$

i.e. $Z_L=Z_S^*$

Maximum Power Transfer Theorem

S. No. $Z_S$ (Source Impedance) $Z_L$ (Load Impedance) Condition for MPT $P_L(\max )$ [Maximum power delivered to load] $$
\% \eta
$$

(Efficiency)
1. $R_S+j 0$ $R_L=R_S$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$ $50 \%$
2. $R_S+j X_S$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+\left(X_S+X_L\right)^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$
3. $R_S+j X_S$ $X_L+X_S=0$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2 R_L}{\left(R_s+R_L\right)^2}$ $<50 \%$
4. $R_S+j X_S$ $Z_L=Z_S^*$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L=\frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$ $50 \%$
5. $R_S+j X_S$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+X_S^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$
6. $R_S+j 0$ $R_L=\sqrt{R_S^2+X_L^2}$ $\left|I^2\right| R_L$ $<50 \%$

content: For the network shown in Fig., evaluate the current I flowing through the 2Ω resistor using superposition theorem.

A voltage source of internal impedance $${\mathrm R}_\mathrm s\;+\;{\mathrm{jX}}_\mathrm s$$ supplies power to a load of impedance $${\mathrm R}_\mathrm L\;+\;{\mathrm{jX}}_\mathrm L$$ in which only $${\mathrm R}_\mathrm L$$ is variable. Determine the value of $${\mathrm R}_\mathrm L$$ for maximum power transfer from the source to the load. Also, find the numerical value of $${\mathrm R}_\mathrm L$$ if the source impedance is 3.0 Ω (purely resistive) and $${\mathrm X}_\mathrm L$$ is 4.0 Ω.
answer:
$${\mathrm R}_\mathrm L\;=\;\sqrt{{\mathrm R}_\mathrm S^2\;+\;\left({\mathrm X}_\mathrm L\;+\;{\mathrm X}_\mathrm S\right)^2}$$
$${\mathrm R}_\mathrm L$$ =5 Ω

content:
In the circuit of Fig. when R = 0 Ω, the current iR equals 10 A.

(a) Find the value of R for which it absorbs maximum power.

(b) Find the value of E.

(c) Find v2 when R = $$\infty$$ ( open circuit)
answer:
(a) R = 2 Ω
(b) E = 64 V
(c) v2 = 6 V

content: In the circuit shown in Fig., it is known that the variable current source I absorbs power.Find I (in magnitude and direction) so that it receives maximum power and also find the amount of power absorbed by it.
answer: I = 0.5 A from A to B, P<sub>max</sub> = 1 W

content: Determine the current, i(t), in the circuit given below, (Fig.), using the Thevenin's theorem.
